levels.body: Biblical: The meaning of Seraphim (pl.) as that of angels: “The living creatures with six wings, hands and feet, and a (presumably) human voice, seen in Isaiah's vision as hovering above the throne of God.” (“Seraphim | Seraphin, *n*.” Sense 1.); meaning class of angels or celestial beings. The derivation is presumably from a Hebrew root meaning ‘to burn’ or ‘to destroy’ (“Seraphim | Seraphin, *n*.” Sense 2).
worksCited: “Seraphim | Seraphin, *n*.” *Oxford English Dictionary*, Oxford UP, December 2025, doi.org/10.1093/OED/4377271610. Accessed 18 Mar. 2026.

levels.body:
Seraphim are the highest choir of the nine orders of angels in Christian angelology, ranked above cherubim (Ezekiel), and below God. In Jewish angelology, the Seraphim are the 5th highest order of angels. In other texts, the Seraphim are also associated with serpents – Egyptian designation – and fire, and with light, charity, ardor and purity (Hartenstein 166; Aquinas 1185-87).

The speaker talks of Seraphim as beings swinging a censer, hearing it striding on the chamber floor. It is likely a metaphor for the speaker noticing a pressing shift in the air concerning both smell and density.
worksCited: Hartenstein, Friedhelm. “Cherubim and Seraphim in the Bible and in the Light of Ancient Near Eastern Sources.” *Deuterocanonical and Cognate Literature Yearbook*, 2007, pp. 155-188, doi.org/10.1515/9783110192957.2.155. Accessed 13 June 2024.

levels.body: In the Book of Isaiah, the passage 6:1-7 provides a first physical description of the Seraph as an entity with wings, flying around the Throne of God and proclaiming God’s holiness and glory. In the poem, the Seraphim burn incense, possibly as part of a cleansing ritual (cf. Level 3 Interpretation and [[perfumed-from-an-unseen-censer]]), relating to a Seraph touching Isaiah’s lips with a piece of coal with the implication of Isaiah having been cleansed in order to speak on God’s behalf: “And I heard the voice of the Lord saying, “Whom shall I send, and who will go for us?” Then I said, “Here I am! Send me.“ (Isa 6:8).
worksCited: *Authorized (King James) Version*. Cambridge UP, Bible Gateway, www.biblegateway.com/passage/?search=Isaiah%206&version=AKJV. Accessed 20 June 2024.

levels.body:
The root meaning of Seraphim being equal to ‘to burn’ can be understood literally in its connection to the censer, thereby the Seraph in its inherent meaning is the one to carry the vessel in which incense is burned. In his *Summa Theologiae*, Thomas Aquinas writes that “[t]he name “Seraphim” does not come from charity only, but from the excess of charity, expressed by the word ardor or fire” (1187) and expresses three interpretations of the fire associated with the Seraphim. One of these interpretations sees the angels as exercising “heat” in an action “upon those who are subject to them, rousing them to a like fervor, and cleansing them wholly by their heat” (1187-88). Since the Seraph “contain[s] an excess of heat” (Aquinas 1887) and is the one swinging the censer with the burning incense, it may be interpreted as a cleansing ritual. Aquinas interprets fire likewise with qualities of brightness and clarity as the Seraphim are filled with an inextinguishable light with which to enlighten others (1188). The presence of the Seraphim can therefore be taken as an instance of enlightenment and clarity.

The speaker considers the Seraphim as celestial beings with which the atmosphere changes to become a hopeful and enlightened instance for the speaker as the suggestion is made that the air feels purified (cf. Level 1 Language [[censer]]). The description of the Seraphim allows for a reading as a bringer of protection as the term “tinkle” within the same line “Swung by Seraphim whose foot-falls tinkled on the tufted floor” lightens the mood and understanding of a Seraph by emphasizing the entrance of the Seraphim to sound melodic. The purpose of the Seraphim is, therefore, to allow the speaker a small reprieve from his felt agony and spark a moment of hope, clarity and enlightenment. The Seraphim relate hope for the speaker, as they embody holiness that correlates to charity, divine mercy and respite, which is what the speaker longs for.

The Seraph has an overall ambiguous in-built interpretation, as another meaning can be derived from its Hebrew origin not just as ‘to burn’ but also as ‘to destroy’, invoking the Seraph as a being that annihilates and incinerates a third object; a causer of chaos ("Seraphim | Seraphin, *n*.", Sense 2). In correlation, Lucifer was identified either as a cherub or a seraph before his fall from grace (Russell 94-95). Additionally, referring back to angelology itself, angels are considered as immensely scary according to the bible, in which the appearance of angels is frequently accompanied by them saying “Be not afraid” (e.g., cf. Lk 1:12-13; Lk 1:30). This adds a horror element of a scary exterior which takes away from an understanding of protection. 
Furthermore, while the speaker cries out for respite from his memories and asks for [[nepenthe]] to help him forget his anguish of losing Lenore, the raven’s utterance of, once again, ‘nevermore’ denies him his relief from anguish and demolishes his hopeful stance. Because of its foreboding nature as the harbinger of despair and ominousness, the speaker views the raven as his tormenter rather than a helping hand, seen in line 81: “Wretch,” I cried, “thy God hath lent thee—by these angels he hath sent thee”. This ominous and eerie atmosphere is further enunciated by the unknown number of Seraphim in the room, densing the atmosphere at hand. Moreover, “by these angels he hath sent thee” (l.81) directly claims the raven to stem from these angels (i.e., a physical manifestation of the Seraph), and since the Seraphim are a metaphorical figure, ambiguously and simultaneously understood as a bringer of enlightenment and a harbinger of doom, the speaker experiences mercy and respite and despair and the same time. The implication of the Seraph having wings and human features, even if not expressly mentioned, correlate to the raven who undergoes several instances of personification (cf. [[decorum]] and [[mien]]).
worksCited: *Authorized (King James) Version*. Cambridge UP, Bible Gateway, www.biblegateway.com/versions/Authorized-King-James-Version-AKJV-Bible/#booklist. Accessed 20 June 2024.
Russell, Jeffrey Burton. *Lucifer: The Devil in the Middle Ages*. Cornell UP, 1986.
“Seraphim | Seraphin, *n*.” *Oxford English Dictionary*, Oxford UP, December 2025, doi.org/10.1093/OED/4377271610. 18 Mar. 2026.
